title: Environment Setup Guide
text: Welcome to the course! This notebook will guide you through setting up your environment to ensure you have all the necessary tools and packages installed. You have two options:

title: Environment Setup Guide
text:
Option 1: Using Google Colab
Google Colab allows you to run Jupyter Notebooks in your browser without any setup on your local machine.
Step 1: Accessing Google Colab

Navigate to Google Colab.
Sign in with your Google account.

Step 2: Mounting Google Drive (Optional)
If you wish to save your work directly to your Google Drive:
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
Step 3: Installing Necessary Packages
Colab comes with many pre-installed packages. However, you might need to install additional ones:
!pip install package_name
Step 4: Setting Up Environment Variables (If Required)
If your project requires specific environment variables:
import os
os.environ['VAR_NAME'] = 'value'

title: Environment Setup Guide
text:
Option 2: Using Local Jupyter Notebook
If you prefer to run notebooks on your local machine:
Step 1: Installing Anaconda
Anaconda is a distribution that includes Python, Jupyter Notebook, and other essential packages.

Download Anaconda from the official website.
Follow the installation instructions for your operating system.

Step 2: Launching Jupyter Notebook
After installation:

Open Anaconda Navigator and launch Jupyter Notebook.
Alternatively, open your terminal or command prompt and run:

jupyter notebook
Step 3: Creating a Virtual Environment (Recommended)
It’s good practice to create a virtual environment for your projects:
conda create --name your_env_name python=3.8
conda activate your_env_name
Step 4: Installing Necessary Packages
With your virtual environment activated:
pip install package_name

title: Environment Setup Guide
text:
Testing Your Setup
To ensure everything is set up correctly, run the following code:
try:
    import numpy
    import pandas
    print("Setup successful!")
except ImportError as e:
    print(f"Setup failed: {e}")

title: Environment Setup Guide
text:
Additional Resources

Jupyter Notebook Official Documentation
Google Colab FAQs


Note: Ensure you have a stable internet connection when using Google Colab. For local setups, regularly update your packages to the latest versions.

title: Intro to Image Analysis
text:
Use Python to analyse visual features in food images.
Code
from skimage import io, color
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

image = io.imread('https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/36/Kale-Bundle.jpg')
gray = color.rgb2gray(image)
plt.imshow(gray, cmap='gray')
plt.title('Grayscale Food Image')
plt.axis('off')
plt.show()

title: Intro to Image Analysis
text:
Exercise
Measure basic colour intensity and variation in a food image (e.g., bread crust or fruit ripeness).

title: Bayesian Methods with PyMC
text:
Estimate posterior distributions using MCMC in nutritional applications.
Code
import pymc as pm
import numpy as np
import arviz as az

data = np.random.normal(loc=9, scale=1, size=100)

with pm.Model() as model:
    mu = pm.Normal('mu', mu=8.5, sigma=1)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal('sigma', sigma=1)
    obs = pm.Normal('obs', mu=mu, sigma=sigma, observed=data)
    trace = pm.sample(1000, tune=500)

az.plot_trace(trace);

title: Bayesian Methods with PyMC
text:
Exercise
Use a Bayesian model to estimate the mean intake of under-reported nutrients.

title: Dashboards and Storytelling with Streamlit
text:

st.bar_chart(data.set_index('age'))

title: Introduction to Qualitative Research
text: Understand what qualitative research is and when to use it.

title: Introduction to Qualitative Research
text:
Key Concepts

Focuses on meaning, experience, and context
Best for exploring how and why questions
Common in public health, behavioural nutrition, sensory science


When to Use

Early exploratory phases
To complement quantitative findings (mixed methods)
When numbers alone don’t tell the full story

title: Introduction to Qualitative Research
text:
Exercise
Identify a research question from your field that would benefit from qualitative methods. Explain why.

title: Data Collection Methods in Qualitative Research
text: Learn how qualitative data are typically collected.

title: Data Collection Methods in Qualitative Research
text:
Common Methods

Interviews (structured, semi-structured, unstructured)
Focus groups
Open-ended survey questions


Ethics

Informed consent
Anonymity and confidentiality
Data storage and transparency

title: Simple Text Analysis in Python
text:
Use code to explore qualitative data (e.g. survey comments).
Code
import pandas as pd
from collections import Counter
comments = [
    "I often skip breakfast",
    "Healthy food is too expensive",
    "I don't know how to cook vegetables"
]
tokens = [word.lower() for c in comments for word in c.split()]
Counter(tokens)

text:

title: Statistical Testing
text:

group1 = stats.norm.rvs(loc=8.5, scale=1, size=30)
group2 = stats.norm.rvs(loc=9.0, scale=1, size=30)
stats.ttest_ind(group1, group2)

title: Regression Analysis
text:
Linear and logistic regression using statsmodels.
Code
import statsmodels.api as sm
X = pd.DataFrame({'age': [20, 30, 40, 50], 'sex': [0, 1, 0, 1]})
X = sm.add_constant(X)
y = [1800, 1900, 2000, 2100]
model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()
model.summary()

title: Python vs R vs Other Tools
text: Understanding when to use Python, R, or another tool is key to becoming a well-rounded data analyst.

title: Python vs R vs Other Tools
text:
Summary Table








Tool
Strengths
Weaknesses




Python
General-purpose, great for ML
Less built-in stats support


R
Excellent for statistics
Less general-purpose


Excel
Ubiquitous, quick overview
Not reproducible or scalable

# Example data

sns.histplot(data['sugar'], kde=True)
plt.title('Daily Sugar Intake')
plt.show()

title: Survival Analysis
text:
Explore time-to-event data with Kaplan-Meier curves.
Code
from lifelines import KaplanMeierFitter
kmf = KaplanMeierFitter()
data = pd.DataFrame({'duration': [5, 6, 6, 2.5, 4], 'event': [1, 0, 0, 1, 1]})
kmf.fit(data['duration'], data['event'])
kmf.plot_survival_function()

title: Power Calculations
text:
Estimate the power of your statistical tests.
Code
from statsmodels.stats.power import TTestIndPower
analysis = TTestIndPower()
analysis.solve_power(effect_size=0.5, nobs1=50, alpha=0.05)

title: Data Transformations
text:
Log transformations and z-scores for normalisation.
Code
import numpy as np
sugar = np.array([20, 22, 18, 30, 28])
sugar_log = np.log(sugar)
(sugar_log - np.mean(sugar_log)) / np.std(sugar_log)

title: Bayesian vs Frequentist Thinking
text:
Compare approaches using real or simulated nutrition data.
Code
from scipy import stats
samples = stats.norm.rvs(loc=10, scale=2, size=100)
mean = np.mean(samples)
conf_int = stats.norm.interval(0.95, loc=mean, scale=stats.sem(samples))
conf_int

title: Cleaning Data
text:
Handling missing values, renaming columns, and fixing data types.
Code
df = pd.DataFrame({'iron': ['8.5', None, '10.2'], 'food': ['Spinach', 'Tofu', 'Kale']})
df['iron'] = pd.to_numeric(df['iron'], errors='coerce')
df = df.rename(columns={'iron': 'iron_mg'})
df

df.head()

title: Multivariate Analysis in Metabolomics and Sensory Science
text:
Apply PCA to high-dimensional food-related datasets.
Code
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X = pd.DataFrame({
    'sucrose': [0.8, 1.1, 0.9, 1.2],
    'glucose': [0.7, 0.6, 0.8, 0.9],
    'fructose': [0.9, 1.0, 0.85, 1.1]
})

X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
components = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)
components

title: Welcome
text:
Welcome to this comprehensive teaching resource to help you develop skills in data handling, programming, and statistical analysis using Python.
All materials are structured to be self-guided and modular, supporting both lectures and independent learning. Each section can be explored individually or followed in sequence.





Understand your data environment, tools, platforms, and version control workflows.



Learn how to write Python code, structure your work, and document it effectively.



Import, clean, transform, and manage datasets from simple to complex formats.



Run statistical tests, build models, and explore results through rich visualisations.



Explore Bayesian methods, databases, automation, dashboards, multivariate and image analysis.



Explore qualitative methods including coding, thematic analysis, and basic NLP in nutrition and public health.



Use templates and mini-projects to practice and demonstrate your knowledge.






Source: GitHub Repository
Rendered Site: ggkuhnle.github.io/data-analysis-toolkit-FNS
Run code using Google Colab, Jupyter, or locally on your machine.


📌 Tip: You can dip into any section independently or follow the structure as a progressive course. All notebooks are open-source and available for reuse with attribution.
